title: Australia GP Winners and Losers: Who left Melbourne up, and who left with questions
meta: Published: 9 March 2026 | F1CC News Race Desk
caption: Lead image: Eetu Väisänen after winning the Australian GP.
body:
Melbourne looked like one race on paper and three races in practice. Eetu Väisänen won from P1, but the Australian Grand Prix was shaped by changing rain intensity, split tire timing, and then a puncture cascade that punished anyone caught on the wrong phase.

The strategic pattern from the race was clear: an early full-wet reset after Julian Kondra's crash, a mid-race crossover gamble to intermediates by selected runners, and a late collapse in wet-tire life when teams waited too long to react.

The decisive sequence: After the safety-car phase, most of the field stayed committed to full wets. When rain eased, Mercedes, Alpine, and Williams were the bold teams that switched to intermediates. At first, that call looked traffic-limited, but once full wets overheated, the inter runners gained quickly and effectively won a pit-stop phase. As heavier rain returned, most teams had to cycle back to fresh wets; in the corrected commentary this shift is called shortly before the puncture phase, roughly in the lap-30 to lap-35 window, with repeated 'rain getting heavier again' and 'grade three rain' warnings. The drivers who tried to stretch their first wet set through the drying phase were the ones most exposed when water load rose, with Konstantaras flagged as the first major trigger before failures spread through the pack.

That sequence explains why the final order diverged so sharply from the running order seen earlier in the race.

Winners

Eetu Väisänen (Ferrari, P1 from P1): He controlled the wet opening, survived the lead changes around the early safety-car cycle, and then stayed out of the chaos when strategy fragmented. In commentary terms, while others were forced into reactive calls and puncture risk, Väisänen was repeatedly described as the benchmark who "has done everything right today" and brought the race home cleanly.

Wesley Vandesteene (Mercedes, P2 from P5): His race was the best example of timing recovery. He initially lost time in the Mercedes pit sequence behind Zwart, then got his result back through the intermediate gamble phase when "the gamble is working" became the defining call. Starting third row and finishing second reframed his race from damage limitation to strategic execution.

Michiil Maradöner (Alpine, P3 from P8): Maradöner's race rose during the same crossover window. The commentary repeatedly highlighted his overtakes once inter pace came alive, and by the time the field reset on wets he had converted that phase into podium track position. From P8, this was an attack race, not a hold race.

Sarah Springfield (Aston Martin, P6 from P18): Her value came from survival and timing. Springfield was explicitly called out as one of the few not to suffer a puncture during the collapse phase, and she converted that stability into a major points gain from deep on the grid.

Puncture survivors in the points: Springfield (P18 to P6), Zhao Lei (P13 to P7), Riley Swansson (P16 to P9), and Lukus Wright (P12 to P10) were repeatedly noted in the pack that emerged with points while others dropped out of contention during puncture chaos. In this race, tire preservation under uncertainty was a strategy result, not just a driving result.

Mercedes as a unit: Even with early pit-sequence complications, Mercedes left with P2 and P5 from P5 and P3 respectively. The team read the crossover window better than most and recovered from early disruption to bank the strongest two-car score profile among direct rivals.

Losers

Afonso Cachinho (Ferrari, P12 from P17): Commentary framed his day as pressure-heavy from the start and reactive in the race. He was later named in the puncture sequence and never recovered to points, which made Ferrari's result profile one-sided despite winning the race. From P17, he needed a clean attrition climb and did not get it.

Red Bull conversion (both cars out of points): Verhaeren qualified P7 but had to start from the pit lane, then recovered to P11, which is a strong salvage drive in isolation. Team-level, though, it still lands as a loser outcome because neither Red Bull scored after showing enough pace to target the top ten.

Robin Keisner and Audi's race management (P15 from P10): Keisner was involved in difficult stop phases and was repeatedly caught in traffic or incident-adjacent moments. Starting in the top ten made this a clear conversion miss rather than a pace discovery weekend.

Joshua Nathanial (McLaren, DNF from P9): His retirement came in the same curb-sensitive, low-grip pattern discussed throughout the wet phases. Once he was out, McLaren's strategy options narrowed to salvage, and only one side of the garage could score.

The puncture victims: The commentary names Konstantaras (started P20) as the first major puncture trigger, then Roy (P7 start), Cachinho (P17 start), and others joining the failure wave as worn wet tires, already overheated during the drier crossover, gave up once heavy rain loaded them again. That phase was the central strategic collapse point of the race.

The teams that missed the timing: This race punished late reactions more than lack of pace. The teams that read the inter window and the return-to-wets window cleanly rose; those that stayed out one phase too long paid with punctures, emergency stops, and lost races.

Round one does not decide titles, but this race already defined the operating rule for 2026: in unstable weather, strategy timing is performance.
